title: Chapter 7: Through the Walls
content: On Day Seven, I started listening.

Not with my ears—I'd been doing that since Day One, flinching at every creak and groan of the building. I mean *really* listening. Applying my gamer's attention to detail to the sounds of Crimson Ridge Complex, mapping the soundscape the way I'd map a dungeon.

The building was alive in a way it had never been before the outbreak. Not with human life—with *something*. The moaning that rose from the lower floors had a rhythm to it, almost tidal, peaking at dusk and dawn. Footsteps shuffled in patterns—some random, some looping the same route over and over, zombies trapped in some ghost of their daily commute. The elevator shaft made sounds I didn't want to investigate.
But the sound that mattered came through the ventilation shaft in my bathroom.

The first time I heard it, I was brushing my teeth with the last of my toothpaste, and I almost swallowed it. A voice. Faint, distorted by the ductwork, but unmistakably human. A woman's voice, low and urgent.
"—I told you, I don't know where they went. They're gone. Everyone on this floor is gone."
A pause. Then a man's voice, louder, rough with something that made my skin crawl:
"You're lying. People don't just disappear. You're hiding something."
"I'm not hiding anything. Please, Derek, I just want to eat. I haven't eaten in two days—"
"You'll eat when I say you eat."
The sound of flesh hitting flesh. A cry. Then silence, except for breathing—one heavy and angry, one shallow and pained.
I stood in my bathroom with toothpaste foam dripping down my chin, every muscle locked, my heart doing its level best to exit through my throat.
Floor 8. Apartment 8A. The blood trail I'd seen. The "Please. Don't." that had haunted me for two days.
This was the source.

I crept to the couch where Luna was dozing with Boss on her lap and shook her shoulder—gently, always gently, because she still startled like a wild animal and I'd already lost one mug to her reflexes.
"Hey," I whispered. "Wake up. I need to show you something."
Her eyes opened instantly—zombies didn't do groggy—and tracked to my face with that searching intensity she always had when I woke her. "What?"
"Listen." I led her to the bathroom and pressed her ear to the vent. Her eyes widened, then narrowed. She could hear better than I could—significantly better—and what she heard made her lip curl.
"Hurt," she said. "Woman. Hurting."
"Yeah." My jaw clenched. "I heard him hit her."

Luna's expression shifted. I'd seen her angry before—at a zombie that had wandered too close to our floor, at a rat that had startled Boss. But this was different. This was *focused* anger, the kind that came from a place deeper than instinct.
"Kill?" she asked. Simple. Direct. A genuine question from someone who'd been dead and understood death as a practical solution.

I wanted to say yes. Some part of me—the part that had been helpless his whole life, the part that had watched people get bullied in school and done nothing, the part that hated itself for every moment of cowardice—wanted to say *yes, kill him, tear him apart, make him stop hurting her.*
But I wasn't that guy. Not yet. Maybe not ever.
"No," I said. "Not yet. We need information first. Who he is, what he has, how we can get her out without getting ourselves killed."
Luna studied me for a long moment. Then she nodded—not agreement, but trust. She trusted me to make this call.
I hoped I deserved that trust.
For the next two days, I listened.

The ventilation system of Crimson Ridge was a highway of secrets. The main duct ran vertically through the building, with branches to each apartment's bathroom and kitchen vents. Sound traveled remarkably well—I could hear conversations on Floor 8 from my bathroom, and with Luna's help, I could even pick up whispers on Floors 7 and 9.
I learned the following:

**Derek Voss**, Apartment 8A. Former personal trainer at the gym across the street. Built like a refrigerator, temper like a lit match. He'd been hoarding supplies since Day One, methodically clearing apartments on his floor and stockpiling food, water, and weapons in his unit. He had a 9mm pistol with limited ammunition, a baseball bat, and a mean right hook that I'd heard deployed three times in two days.

**Sarah Mitchell**, also Apartment 8A. Not a resident—she'd been visiting a friend on Floor 8 when the outbreak hit, and Derek had "taken her in." That was his phrasing, overheard through the vent: "I took you in. I protect you. You owe me." She'd been with him since Day One, and the pattern was the same every day: he demanded information about other survivors (she didn't have any), he rationed her food (barely enough to stay conscious), and when he drank—which was every night, from a stockpile of whiskey he'd looted—he got handsy, and then he got violent.
She never screamed. That was the worst part. She'd flinch, gasp, go quiet, but she never screamed. Like she'd learned long before the apocalypse that screaming only made it worse.
My watch was vibrating constantly now.

**QUEST UPDATE: Rescue and Romance Sarah Mitchell**
**AFFECTION: 0% (Target has not met Host)**
**URGENCY: CRITICAL**
**QUEST TIMER: 23:47:22**
**BONUS REWARD PREVIEW: Titanium Alloy Safe House (Legendary Grade)**
Legendary grade. The system had never shown me that tier before. Luna's rewards had been "Uncommon" and "Rare." This was *Legendary*—the kind of reward that changed the entire game.
But the reward wasn't why I was going down there.
I was going down there because I'd heard a woman say "please don't" and I'd done nothing for three days, and I couldn't live with that anymore.
"Luna," I said, sitting down next to her on the couch. "I need to tell you something, and I need you to not kill me for it."
She tilted her head. Waiting.
"I'm going to Floor 8 tomorrow. I'm going to get that woman out of there."
Her hand found mine. Cold. Strong.
"Go," she said. "Together."
"Together," I agreed.
I looked at my watch. At the countdown burning away in blue-white numbers. At the reward preview that promised a titanium fortress if I could save one woman from one monster.
Then I looked at Luna, who'd chosen the word *stay* as her first human word, and who was now choosing *together*.
I wasn't a hero. I was a gamer with a magic watch and a zombie girlfriend and a cat named Boss.
But sometimes, you don't need to be a hero. You just need to show up.
Tomorrow, I'd show up.
Tonight, I held Luna's hand and listened to the silence of a building full of the dead, and somewhere in that silence, a living woman breathed.
